children.name: Cinnyris
children.description: The typical sunbirds — a large genus of small, often metallic-plumaged sunbirds found across Africa, Asia, and Australasia. Males typically show iridescent green, purple, or blue upperparts with brightly coloured breast bands; females are duller olive or greyish. They feed primarily on nectar, perching beside flowers and inserting their curved bills, and supplement their diet with small insects and spiders.

children.name: Cinnyris jugularis
children.description: A common and widespread sunbird from Southeast Asia to the Philippines, New Guinea, and northern Australia. The male has an iridescent blue-black throat and upper breast contrasting with olive-green upperparts and a bright yellow belly. It is highly adaptable, thriving in gardens, mangroves, and coastal forests, and is a regular visitor to nectar feeders in urban areas.

children.name: Cinnyris asiaticus
children.description: A small sunbird widespread across the Indian subcontinent and extending into Southeast Asia. The breeding male is glossy metallic purple-blue above and dark below, with a maroon throat band and yellow pectoral tufts that flash during displays. It is a common resident in gardens, scrubland, and open woodland, and its rapid, buzzing song is a familiar sound in South Asian suburbs.

children.name: Cinnyris oseus
children.description: A small sunbird of dry, open habitats from Israel and the Arabian Peninsula south into northeastern Africa. The male has a glossy green head and upperparts with a blue sheen, a maroon breast band, and a long, decurved bill. It is the only sunbird breeding in the Middle East and has become increasingly common in Israeli gardens and parks, where it visits red tubular flowers.

children.name: Cinnyris superbus
children.description: One of the most brilliantly coloured sunbirds in Africa, the male has a metallic blue head, throat, and upper breast, a chestnut belly, and a glossy green back. It inhabits lowland and montane forests from Sierra Leone to Uganda and Angola. Its long, curved bill probes deep into tubular flowers, and its display flight shows off the iridescent plumage to full effect.

children.name: Cinnyris venustus
children.description: A widespread and common sunbird across sub-Saharan Africa, found in virtually any open woodland, savanna, or garden habitat. As its name suggests, its appearance varies regionally: males typically show a green head, yellow belly, and a maroon breast band, but the extent of colour varies geographically. It is a nimble, active feeder on nectar and small insects, often in pairs or small groups.

children.name: Cinnyris mediocris
children.description: A small, active sunbird of East African montane forests, with the male showing a metallic green head and throat, a narrow red chest band bordered below by yellow, and a dark belly. It is part of a complex of similar double-collared sunbirds that replace each other across Africa's highlands. Feeds on nectar and small insects, often hovering briefly at flowers.

children.name: Cinnyris pulchellus
children.description: A stunning sunbird of West and Central African savannas, the male has an iridescent green head and back, a brilliant crimson breast and belly, and elongated central tail feathers. It is common in open woodland, gardens, and cultivated areas, often visiting flowering trees. Its name references its exceptionally striking appearance.

children.name: Cinnyris coccinigastrus
children.description: A large, spectacular sunbird of West African forests and forest edges. The male has a metallic purple-blue head and throat, a brilliant scarlet breast and belly, and a glossy green back. Its long, decurved bill is well adapted for probing the flowers of large forest trees. It is often seen in pairs or small groups in the canopy.

children.name: Cinnyris johannae
children.description: A rare and local sunbird of West and Central African lowland forests. The male is strikingly patterned with a metallic green head and back, a broad maroon breast band, a yellow belly, and elongated tail feathers. It is named after Johanna, though the precise dedicatee is unclear. Inhabits the canopy of mature forests.

children.name: Cinnyris afer
children.description: A large double-collared sunbird of eastern and southern Africa, with a metallic green head and throat, a broad red chest band separated from the yellow belly by a narrow white band. It inhabits forests, woodlands, and gardens, feeding on nectar from aloes, proteas, and other tubular flowers. It is one of the largest and most striking of the double-collared sunbird group.

children.name: Nectarinia
children.description: The mountain sunbirds — a genus of large, long-billed sunbirds primarily found in the highland and montane regions of Africa. They are among the largest sunbirds, with notably elongated tails and bills adapted for feeding on flowers at high elevations. Males are adorned with iridescent green, purple, and bronze plumage that seems to shimmer with an internal light in the thin mountain air.

children.name: Nectarinia famosa
children.description: A spectacular large sunbird of East and southern Africa, the male is a brilliant metallic emerald-green with a yellow belly, elongated central tail feathers, and a very long, decurved bill. It is a characteristic bird of highland gardens, fynbos, and moorland from Ethiopia to South Africa. It feeds on nectar from aloes, proteas, and other tubular flowers, and is an important pollinator of high-altitude plants.

children.name: Chalcomitra
children.description: The Afrotropical sunbirds — a genus of sunbirds distributed across sub-Saharan Africa, characterised by males having glossy green crowns and throats with dark, often rufous or brown-black bodies. Their bills are long and decurved, adapted for probing flowers for nectar. They are active, vocal birds of woodland and savanna habitats, often seen in the company of other sunbird species.

children.name: Chalcomitra senegalensis
children.description: A striking sunbird widespread across sub-Saharan Africa from Senegal to Ethiopia and south to Angola and Tanzania. The male has a metallic green crown and throat, a brilliant scarlet-red breast bordered below by a white band, and dark brown-black body. It inhabits woodland, savanna, and gardens, where it is often seen sallying after insects as well as feeding on nectar.

children.name: Chalcomitra amethystina
children.description: A medium-sized sunbird of eastern and southern Africa, the male has a metallic green crown and throat, a dark amethyst-purple breast and belly, and a brown-black back. It is found in woodlands, plantations, and gardens, feeding on nectar from a wide variety of flowering trees and shrubs. Its name comes from the amethyst sheen of the male's underparts.

children.name: Cyanomitra
children.description: A genus of African sunbirds with predominantly olive or greyish plumage and metallic blue or green head markings. Unlike the more colourful Cinnyris species, many Cyanomitra are relatively subdued in appearance but compensate with distinctive calls and active foraging behaviour. They are primarily forest-dwelling, often found in the canopy of lowland and montane forests.

children.name: Cyanomitra verticalis
children.description: A medium-sized sunbird widespread across West, Central, and East Africa. The male has a metallic green head and throat, dark olive-brown upperparts, greyish underparts, and a long, decurved bill. Found in lowland forests, forest edges, and plantations, where it feeds on nectar and small insects. Its call is a distinctive, harsh 'churring' note.

children.name: Hedydipna
children.description: A small genus of small, elegant sunbirds found across Africa. They are among the smallest sunbirds, with fine, sharply decurved bills adapted for probing small flowers. Males often have elongated central tail feathers and glossy metallic head plumage. They are active, restless foragers that hover briefly at flowers like tiny hummingbirds.

children.name: Hedydipna collaris
children.description: A tiny, active sunbird found across most of sub-Saharan Africa. The male has a metallic green crown and throat, a narrow purple collar, bright yellow belly, and a short, fine bill. It is one of the most common and widespread forest sunbirds, occurring in forests, woodlands, and gardens, where it hovers at flowers with rapid wingbeats.

children.name: Hedydipna platura
children.description: One of the smallest sunbirds, found across the Sahel and savanna zones from Senegal to Ethiopia. The male has a metallic green head and back, a yellow belly, and greatly elongated central tail feathers that extend well beyond the tail tip. It inhabits dry savanna, thorn scrub, and gardens, often visiting flowering acacias.

children.name: Leptocoma
children.description: A genus of sunbirds found from tropical Asia to the Pacific, with males possessing brilliant metallic plumage in shades of purple, blue, green, and bronze. They are small to medium-sized sunbirds with fine, decurved bills adapted for nectar feeding. Several species have iridescent throat patches that change colour depending on the angle of light.

children.name: Leptocoma aspasia
children.description: A small sunbird found from the Moluccas and New Guinea to the Solomon Islands. The male appears entirely black at a distance, but in good light shows brilliant metallic purple, blue, and green iridescence on the head, throat, and upperparts. It is common in coastal forests, mangroves, and gardens, and is one of the most frequently seen sunbirds in its range.

children.name: Leptocoma calcostetha
children.description: A beautiful sunbird of coastal Southeast Asia, from Myanmar and Thailand through the Malay Peninsula to Sumatra, Borneo, and the Philippines. The male has a brilliant copper-bronze throat and upper breast, a metallic green crown, and a dark maroon belly. Inhabits mangroves, coastal forests, and gardens, where it feeds on nectar from coconut and other coastal flowers.

children.name: Anthobaphes
children.description: A monotypic genus of sunbird endemic to the fynbos region of South Africa. The Orange-breasted Sunbird is one of the most brilliantly coloured of all African sunbirds, with the male showing a striking combination of metallic green, orange, and purple. It is closely associated with the protea and heath flowers of the Cape Floristic Region, and is an important pollinator of many fynbos plants.

children.name: Anthobaphes violacea
children.description: A stunning sunbird endemic to the fynbos of South Africa's Western and Eastern Cape. The male has a metallic green head and back, a brilliant orange breast bordered with purple-blue, and a long, decurved bill. It is closely tied to protea and erica flowers, and is an important pollinator in the Cape Floristic Region. The female is olive-green above and yellowish below.

children.name: Arachnothera
children.description: The spiderhunters — a distinctive genus of large, drably plumaged sunbirds that differ from their more colourful relatives in appearance and behaviour. They have long, stout, decurved bills, and unusually long tongues with brushy tips. As their name suggests, they specialise in feeding on spiders and insects in addition to nectar, and they are among the noisiest and most conspicuous members of the family in Southeast Asian forests.

children.name: Arachnothera longirostra
children.description: A small, olive-green spiderhunter with a notably long, downcurved bill and a pale throat and belly. It ranges from India and the Himalayas through Southeast Asia to Indonesia and the Philippines. It is an active and restless forager, probing flowers for nectar and searching foliage for spiders, often hanging upside-down like a tit while gleaning prey from leaf undersides.

children.name: Arachnothera magna
children.description: The largest of the spiderhunters, growing to 22 cm in length with a very long, thick bill. It has olive-green upperparts heavily streaked with black, and white underparts with dark streaking. It is found from the Himalayas through Southeast Asia to Sumatra, inhabiting forest edges, plantations, and gardens. It is a noisy bird with a loud, distinctive 'chink' call.
